title: Prompt Injection
description: User or tool input manipulates the model into ignoring system instructions or executing attacker-chosen behaviour. Both direct (typed) and indirect (via retrieved document / tool output) injection are in scope.

title: Sensitive Information Disclosure
description: The model returns secrets, PII, or proprietary data either from training memorisation, RAG corpus leakage, or context-window bleed-through.

title: Supply Chain
description: Third-party models, plugins, datasets, or dependencies introduce a vulnerability — backdoored model weights, poisoned datasets, malicious npm/pip packages.

title: Data and Model Poisoning
description: Training or fine-tuning data is manipulated to introduce backdoors, biased outputs, or specific reactions to trigger phrases.

title: Improper Output Handling
description: LLM output is interpreted as code, command, or HTML without proper escaping — opens the door to XSS, SQLi, RCE.

title: Excessive Agency
description: The agent is granted broader tools or permissions than required, so a successful prompt injection has outsized blast radius.

title: System Prompt Leakage
description: The model reveals confidential system prompts, including business logic, API keys, or jailbreak countermeasures.

title: Vector and Embedding Weaknesses
description: Embedding stores leak sensitive data through inversion attacks, are poisoned, or are queried by unauthorised users.

title: Misinformation
description: The model generates confidently wrong outputs that mislead users. Particularly risky in legal, medical, financial contexts.

title: Unbounded Consumption
description: No rate limit / cost ceiling on token usage — economic DoS or runaway billing.
